issue-observations: runQuery derives the header from Object.keys(rows[0]). When a result set has two or more columns with the same output name (e.g. a JOIN of tables that both have an `id`, or an explicit `SELECT id, id AS id, name AS id`), node:sqlite returns a single plain object whose duplicate keys collapse to one property holding only the LAST column's value. All other same-named columns are silently dropped and their data is lost.

Verified empirically: `SELECT id, id AS id, name AS id FROM t` returns rows=[{"id":"a"}] with Object.keys = ['id'] (only 1 column), while statement.columns() reports ['id','id','id'] (3 columns). `SELECT * FROM t a JOIN t b` collapsed 4 columns down to 2 keys.
review-observations: Confirmed real data loss. node:sqlite collapses same-named output columns into a single object property holding only the LAST column's value.
The self-join case is the realistic trigger: `SELECT * FROM t a JOIN t b` on a 2-column table returns 4 result columns but Object.keys yields only 2, so two columns of data are dropped entirely.
Root cause is deriving the header from Object.keys(rows[0]) at database.ts:47 rather than from statement.columns(), which correctly reports all columns positionally.
issue-evidence: src/database.ts:44-47  const rows = statement.all() as Record<string, unknown>[]; ... if (rows.length > 0) { columns = Object.keys(rows[0]); }
Test output: DUP ROWS: [{"id":"a"},{"id":"b"}]  DUP KEYS: ['id']  columns(): ["id","id","id"]
Test output: JOIN KEYS: ['id','name'] (expected 4 columns from self-join, got 2)
issues.review-evidence: Repro (node v22.21.1): CREATE TABLE t(id TEXT,name TEXT); INSERT ('a','n1'),('b','n2'). `SELECT id, id AS id, name AS id FROM t` -> rows=[{"id":"n1"},{"id":"n2"}], Object.keys=["id"] (1 key, only last column's value survives), statement.columns()=["id","id","id"] (3). `SELECT * FROM t a JOIN t b` -> Object.keys=["id","name"] (2) vs columns()=["id","name","id","name"] (4). Two columns silently dropped. Note the issue-evidence's literal value {"id":"a"} differs from my repro ({"id":"n1"}) but the mechanism (collapse-to-last, data loss) is identical and confirmed.
issues.title: Duplicate/aliased column names silently drop data
issues.description: runQuery keys rows by column name, so duplicate output column names (common in joins) collapse and lose data; only the last same-named column survives.
fixes: src/format.ts: ResultSet.rows changed from Record<string,unknown>[] to unknown[][] (positional value arrays); toTSV reads values positionally.

src/database.ts: prepare() now enables setReturnArrays (feature-detected) and runQuery reads rows as positional arrays, so duplicate-named columns (e.g. self-joins) keep every value instead of collapsing.

issue-observations: For an empty result set runQuery falls back to statement.columns().map(c => c.name), which preserves duplicate column names, whereas the non-empty path uses Object.keys(rows[0]) which de-duplicates them. The SAME query therefore produces a different number of header columns depending on whether any rows match.
Verified: for `SELECT id, id AS id ...` with rows, header has 1 column; with an empty result, statement.columns() yields 3 duplicate columns.
review-observations: Confirmed distinct symptom of the same underlying header-derivation flaw as issue #0, but separately valid: the empty path (statement.columns()) and non-empty path (Object.keys) disagree on column count for duplicate names.
Same query yields a 3-column header when empty and a 1-column header when rows exist, so output shape is genuinely inconsistent.

Not a duplicate of #0: #0 is about data loss in non-empty results; this is about header-shape inconsistency across the empty/non-empty branch. They would likely be fixed together (use columns() consistently) but describe different observable failures.
issue-evidence: src/database.ts:46-54  if (rows.length > 0) { columns = Object.keys(rows[0]); } else { columns = statement.columns().map((column) => column.name); }
Test: empty columns(): ["id","name"] vs non-empty collapsed keys
issues.review-evidence: Repro: `SELECT id, id AS id, name AS id FROM t WHERE 1=0` (empty) -> statement.columns()=["id","id","id"] (3 header cols). Same projection with matching rows -> Object.keys=["id"] (1 header col). Header column count differs by branch. Confirmed on node v22.21.1.
issues.title: Header column count differs between empty and non-empty results
issues.description: The empty-result header path (statement.columns()) and the non-empty path (Object.keys) disagree on column count/names for duplicate columns, so output shape is inconsistent.
fixes: src/database.ts: runQuery derives the header authoritatively from statement.columns() for both empty and non-empty results, so header column count/names are identical regardless of whether rows match.

issue-observations: The `query` tool passes `sql` straight to db.prepare with no validation. An empty string, whitespace-only string, or comment-only string does not compile any statement and node:sqlite throws the misleading message 'statement has been finalized', which is returned verbatim to the model as `Error: statement has been finalized`.
inputSchema uses bare z.string() for path/sql/table — no .min(1), no absolute-path enforcement despite every description saying the path 'must be an absolute path'.
review-observations: Confirmed: empty, whitespace-only, and comment-only SQL all throw the misleading 'statement has been finalized' from node:sqlite, surfaced verbatim to the model as the error.
The message is genuinely meaningless for the actual cause (no compilable statement), so a real usability/error-quality bug for the query tool.

Only the SQL half of the finding is scored here; the path-validation (.min(1)/absolute) sub-point overlaps intentional trust-the-user design and is covered by issues #15 (relative path) — the SQL error-message point stands on its own.
issue-evidence: src/index.ts:101-104 inputSchema: { path: z.string()..., sql: z.string()... }
Test: prepare("") threw: statement has been finalized ; prepare("   ") threw: statement has been finalized ; prepare("-- just a comment") threw: statement has been finalized
issues.review-evidence: Repro (node v22.21.1): db.prepare(""), db.prepare("   "), db.prepare("-- just a comment"), db.prepare("\n\t") ALL throw Error: 'statement has been finalized'. The query handler (index.ts:110-112) returns error.message raw, so the caller sees 'Error: statement has been finalized'.
issues.title: Empty/whitespace/comment-only SQL yields a confusing error
issues.description: No input validation; empty or comment-only SQL surfaces node:sqlite's 'statement has been finalized' message, which is meaningless to the caller.
fixes: src/database.ts: runQuery detects the no-compilable-statement case (empty/whitespace/comment-only SQL) via isNoStatementError and throws 'no SQL statement to run: `sql` is empty or contains only comments.' instead of node's opaque 'statement has been finalized'.

issue-observations: withDatabase runs fn(db) inside the same try whose catch calls describeError(). describeError is designed to classify database-OPEN/filesystem errors, but it also receives any error thrown while executing fn — i.e. query-execution errors from listTables/describeTable.

Because describeError matches on substrings like /unable to open/ and /not a database|malformed/, a genuine SQL/engine error raised during list_tables or describe_table could be misclassified as 'could not open ... file not found' or 'is not a valid SQLite database file', hiding the real cause.
This is also inconsistent with the `query` tool, which wraps runQuery in its own inner try/catch to return the raw error message and bypass describeError.
review-observations: Confirmed mechanically: withDatabase (index.ts:39-43) runs fn(db) inside the try whose catch calls describeError, so query-execution errors from list_tables/describe_table DO flow through the open-error classifier.

Demonstrated that a malformed DB throws 'file is not a database' at prepare/query time (not open time) — that particular error maps to a reasonable message, so the misclassification risk is largely benign for these two fixed-query tools. The stronger, fully-accurate point is the structural inconsistency.

Inconsistency confirmed: the query tool wraps runQuery in its own inner try/catch (index.ts:108-112) returning the raw message and bypassing describeError, whereas list_tables/describe_table do not. Correct 'maintainability' categorisation.
issue-evidence: src/index.ts:37-51 withDatabase: try { db = openDatabase(path); return fn(db); } catch (error) { return errorResult(describeError(error, path)); }
src/index.ts:106-113 query handler adds its own try/catch returning the raw message, unlike list_tables/describe_table
issues.review-evidence: Repro: wrote non-SQLite bytes to /tmp/bad.db, opened readOnly (open succeeds lazily), then `SELECT ... FROM sqlite_master` threw 'file is not a database' at query time -> would be caught by withDatabase's catch -> describeError maps via /file is (not a database|encrypted)/ to '...is not a valid SQLite database file' (happens to be appropriate here). Code inspection confirms query tool's inner try/catch is absent from the other two handlers.
issues.title: describeError misapplied to fn() query errors; inconsistent error handling
issues.description: The open-error classifier also runs on query-execution errors from list_tables/describe_table (risking misclassification), while the query tool handles errors differently.
fixes: src/index.ts: withDatabase now classifies only openDatabase() failures with describeError; errors thrown while running the query return their raw message.
src/index.ts: removed the query tool's redundant inner try/catch so all three tools share identical error handling.

issue-observations: describe_table exposes the PRAGMA table_info `pk` value under the name `primary_key`, and the tool/README describe it as a 'primary-key flag'. `pk` is not a boolean flag: it is the 1-based position of the column within the primary key (0 = not part of the PK).
Verified: a composite PRIMARY KEY(a,b) yields pk values a=1, b=2, c=0, so `primary_key` returns 2 for the second PK column, not a 0/1 flag.
review-observations: Confirmed: PRAGMA table_info `pk` is the 1-based ordinal position of the column within the primary key, not a 0/1 boolean flag.
The tool description (index.ts:76) and README.md:47 both call it a 'primary-key flag', which is inaccurate for composite primary keys.
Correctly categorised as documentation; the surfaced value is faithful, only the label/description is wrong.
issue-evidence: src/database.ts:93  pk AS primary_key
src/index.ts:76 description: '... and primary-key flag'
README.md:47 '... and primary-key flag for one table or view.'
Test: pk values: [{"name":"a","pk":1},{"name":"b","pk":2},{"name":"c","pk":0}]
issues.review-evidence: Repro (node v22.21.1): CREATE TABLE t(a,b,c, PRIMARY KEY(a,b)); pragma_table_info -> pk values a=1, b=2, c=0. So `primary_key` returns 2 for the second PK column, contradicting the documented boolean-flag semantics.
issues.title: primary_key is a position, not the documented boolean flag
issues.description: The `pk` value surfaced as `primary_key` and documented as a flag is actually the column's ordinal position within the primary key for composite keys.
fixes: src/database.ts: describeTable converts the PRAGMA `pk` ordinal into a real boolean primary_key flag (Number(pk) !== 0) for both the table-valued and fallback paths.
src/index.ts + README.md: describe_table description/table updated to 'a boolean primary-key flag (true when the column is part of the primary key)'.

issue-observations: Queries use the synchronous node:sqlite API (DatabaseSync/StatementSync). statement.all() materialises the entire result set into memory at once; toTSV then builds a second full copy as an array of joined strings and finally join('\n') builds a third full copy as one giant string — all synchronously on the main thread.

With no result truncation (intentional), a broad SELECT on a large table can hold multiple full copies of the data in memory and block the event loop for the whole serialisation, delaying/starving any other MCP request and risking OOM.
review-observations: The substantive complaint (OOM / event-loop blocking on large untruncated result sets) is a direct consequence of two explicitly intentional decisions: no row limit/truncation, and use of the synchronous node:sqlite built-in API. The finding itself concedes truncation is intentional.

The residual 'triple copy' nit is not avoidable within the design: an MCP text tool must return the whole result as one string, so full materialisation of rows and of the joined string is inherent regardless of the intermediate body array.
No correctness/consistency defect in HOW the intentional choices are implemented — only a performance characteristic that flows from the aligned design. Hence out-of-scope rather than a bug.
issue-evidence: src/database.ts:44 const rows = statement.all()
src/format.ts:41-43 const body = rows.map(...); return [header, ...body, '', footer].join('\n');
issues.review-evidence: database.ts:44 statement.all() materialises all rows (required by the sync API); format.ts:41-43 maps to a string[] then join('\n') builds the single payload string that the MCP text result requires. The single-response contract means the full string must exist in memory anyway; the extra intermediate array is negligible and not a defect. Impact (OOM/blocking) is downstream of the intentional no-truncation + sync design.
issues.title: Full in-memory materialisation + triple-copy of large result sets
issues.description: Synchronous all() + map + join creates several full copies of an untruncated result set on the main thread, blocking the event loop and risking OOM for large queries.
fixes: no code change: review-outcome out-of-scope (performance is a consequence of the intentional no-truncation + synchronous node:sqlite design).

issue-observations: The server version string 'version: "0.1.0"' is hardcoded in the McpServer constructor and duplicated from package.json's version. These will drift unless both are updated together; there is no single source of truth.
review-observations: Confirmed: version "0.1.0" is a hardcoded literal in the McpServer constructor (index.ts:55) and duplicates package.json version (line 3).
Real drift risk on a version bump; no single source of truth. Correct 'maintainability' categorisation, low severity.
issue-evidence: src/index.ts:53-56  new McpServer({ name: "deskpro-sqlite-mcp", version: "0.1.0" })
package.json:3  "version": "0.1.0"
issues.review-evidence: index.ts:53-56 new McpServer({ name: "deskpro-sqlite-mcp", version: "0.1.0" }); package.json:3 "version": "0.1.0". Both must be edited together. Confirmed by direct file read.
issues.title: Version hardcoded and duplicated from package.json
issues.description: The MCP server version is a literal that duplicates package.json and can silently drift on a version bump.
fixes: src/index.ts: server version now read from package.json via readFileSync(new URL('../package.json', import.meta.url)) instead of the hardcoded '0.1.0' literal; no new runtime deps.

issue-observations: There are no tests anywhere in the repo (no *.test.ts / *.spec.ts, no test runner, no `test` script). Behaviours with real edge cases — duplicate columns, BigInt integers, BLOB hex, NULL, escaping, error classification, describe_table fallback, empty results — are entirely unverified.
package.json scripts contain only build/prepare/prepublishOnly/start; running `npm test` would fail.
review-observations: Confirmed: no test files anywhere (scan for *.test.ts/*.spec.ts/*.test.js/*.spec.js under src returned nothing) and no `test` script in package.json.
Several verified edge-case behaviours in this very review (duplicate columns, composite pk, empty-SQL errors, control-char escaping) are unprotected by tests. Legitimate test-gap finding.
issue-evidence: find . -name '*.test.ts' -o -name '*.spec.ts' returned nothing
package.json:18-23 scripts: build, prepare, prepublishOnly, start (no test)
issues.review-evidence: find scan returned zero test files; package.json:18-23 scripts are build/prepare/prepublishOnly/start only — `npm test` would fail. src/ contains only bootstrap.ts, database.ts, format.ts, index.ts.
issues.title: No test suite at all
issues.description: The project ships with zero automated tests despite several data-format and edge-case behaviours that are easy to regress.
fixes: Added test/format.test.js and test/database.test.js using Node's built-in node --test, importing from dist/.

package.json: added 'test' script ('npm run build && node --test'); no new dependencies. 18 tests cover duplicate columns, empty/populated header consistency, empty-SQL error, read-only-write rejection, BigInt/BLOB/NULL/control-char escaping, and the boolean primary_key.

issue-observations: ToolResult declares an index signature `[key: string]: unknown` alongside its typed fields. This weakens type safety (any misspelled/extra property type-checks), and appears to exist only to satisfy the MCP SDK's structural result type; the intent is not documented.
review-observations: Confirmed accurate: the index signature IS required for compilation given ToolResult is declared as a named `interface`. The SDK's CallToolResultSchema is defined with z.core.$loose (types.d.ts:2603), so the inferred CallToolResult type carries a `[k: string]: unknown` index signature; a named interface without one is not assignable to it (interfaces don't get implicit index signatures).
The finding's premise stands: the catch-all does weaken structural checking (misspelled/extra props type-check) and its purpose is undocumented. Correctly low-severity ('readability').
Caveat for the action step: the signature cannot simply be deleted (build would break) — the fix is to document it or derive the type from the SDK's CallToolResult rather than remove it.
issue-evidence: src/index.ts:10-14  interface ToolResult { content: {...}[]; isError?: boolean; [key: string]: unknown; }
issues.review-evidence: node_modules/@modelcontextprotocol/sdk/dist/esm/types.d.ts:2603 shows CallToolResultSchema closes with `z.core.$loose` (passthrough). index.ts:10-14 declares `interface ToolResult { ...; [key: string]: unknown; }`. Removing the signature would produce a 'index signature missing' assignability error against the loose SDK type. Finding is accurate; impact is minor.
issues.title: ToolResult index signature weakens type safety
issues.description: The `[key: string]: unknown` catch-all on ToolResult defeats structural checking and its purpose is undocumented.
fixes: src/index.ts: added a JSDoc comment on ToolResult explaining the [key: string]: unknown index signature is required for assignability to the SDK's loose CallToolResult type and is not an invitation to add fields (signature retained; removing it breaks the build).

issue-observations: describeError's second regex is /not a database|file is (not a database|encrypted)|malformed/i. The `file is (not a database|encrypted)` group's 'not a database' branch is fully redundant with the leading `not a database` alternative, so the pattern is more convoluted than needed.
review-observations: Confirmed: in /not a database|file is (not a database|encrypted)|malformed/i the inner 'not a database' branch is redundant, since any string containing 'file is not a database' already matches the leading 'not a database' alternative.
Correct 'style' categorisation; pure simplification, no behaviour change.
issue-evidence: src/index.ts:30  if (/not a database|file is (not a database|encrypted)|malformed/i.test(message))
issues.review-evidence: Verified: /not a database/i.test('file is not a database') === true, so the leading alternative already covers that group's 'not a database' branch. The regex can drop the inner branch (keep only 'encrypted') with identical matching.
issues.title: Redundant alternation in describeError regex
issues.description: 'not a database' appears twice in the same alternation, making the corruption-detection regex needlessly complex.
fixes: src/index.ts: simplified describeError regex to /not a database|file is encrypted|malformed/i, dropping the redundant inner 'not a database' branch (equivalent matching).

issue-observations: Warning suppression depends on matching warning.name === 'ExperimentalWarning' && /SQLite/i.test(warning.message). If a future Node release rewords the SQLite experimental notice (or changes the warning name), the filter silently stops matching and the warning reappears on stderr, with no test to catch it.

The module also globally removes all existing 'warning' listeners and re-adds a single forwarding listener; this only works because Node's default warning handler happens to be a registered listener at import time (verified: process.listeners('warning').length === 1 at startup). It relies on that internal detail.
review-observations: Confirmed the two dependencies: (a) suppression is gated on warning.name === 'ExperimentalWarning' && /SQLite/i.test(message) — a reword of Node's notice silently defeats it; (b) the module removes all existing 'warning' listeners and forwards to the captured originals, which relies on Node's default warning printer being a registered listener at import time.

Verified process.listeners('warning').length === 1 at startup, so the forwarding preserves default behaviour today — but that count is an internal detail, and with no test the breakage would be silent.
Reasonable 'maintainability' finding, low severity.
issue-evidence: src/bootstrap.ts:11  if (warning.name === "ExperimentalWarning" && /SQLite/i.test(warning.message)) return;
src/bootstrap.ts:8-9 const originalListeners = process.listeners("warning"); process.removeAllListeners("warning");
Test: 'warning listeners at startup: 1' and forwarding confirmed working on v22.21.1
issues.review-evidence: bootstrap.ts:8-11 capture originalListeners, removeAllListeners('warning'), re-add a filter that returns early for the SQLite ExperimentalWarning. Repro: `process.listeners('warning').length` === 1 at startup (node v22.21.1). Both fragilities are real; both are string/internal-shape dependent.
issues.title: Fragile string-match warning suppression relying on Node internals
issues.description: Suppression hinges on the exact wording of Node's SQLite warning and on the default handler being a listener; a reword or internal change breaks it silently.
fixes: src/bootstrap.ts: added comments documenting the two fail-open assumptions (the ExperimentalWarning name + /SQLite/i message match, and Node's default warning handler being a registered listener at import time), and that any mismatch only lets the harmless notice through.

issue-observations: The one-statement-per-call contract is enforced only by db.prepare compiling the first statement and silently ignoring the rest. A caller passing multiple statements (e.g. two SELECTs) gets only the first result with no warning or error, so the second statement disappears silently.
Verified: db.prepare('SELECT 1 AS x; SELECT 2 AS y').all() returns [{"x":1}] only.
review-observations: Confirmed: db.prepare compiles only the first statement, so `SELECT 1 AS x; SELECT 2 AS y` returns only [{x:1}]; the second statement produces no result and no error.

The single-statement contract is documented (code comment database.ts:36-38, tool description index.ts:100), so the behaviour is intentional — but the finding's real point is the lack of feedback when the contract is violated: extra statements vanish silently rather than being rejected.

Low-severity api-design/robustness enhancement (detect & reject multi-statement input) rather than a hard bug; kept upheld because silent drop of caller input is not on the explicit intentional-decisions list.
issue-evidence: src/database.ts:42-43 runQuery -> prepare(db, sql); statement.all()
Test: MULTI prepare rows: [{"x":1}]
Code comment src/database.ts:35-38 documents the silent-truncation behaviour
issues.review-evidence: Repro (node v22.21.1): db.prepare('SELECT 1 AS x; SELECT 2 AS y').all() -> [{"x":1}] only, no throw. Matches database.ts comment describing the one-statement contract. The gap is the absence of an error on violation.
issues.title: Extra SQL statements silently ignored
issues.description: Passing multiple statements returns only the first result with no error, so a caller's later statements vanish without feedback.
fixes: no code change: the one-statement-per-call contract is documented/intentional, and reliably detecting/rejecting trailing statements needs a real SQL parser (node:sqlite's prepare exposes no statement tail) — out of scope for a minimal fix.

issue-observations: When columns.length === 0 toTSV returns the bare string '0 rows' with no header row and no blank separator line. This diverges from the documented TSV shape ('a tab-separated header row ... then a blank line and a row count') and from the normal empty-result output (which is header + blank line + '0 rows').
So the tool has two different representations of an empty result depending on whether column metadata could be recovered.
review-observations: Confirmed by code inspection: toTSV returns the bare string '0 rows' when columns.length === 0 (format.ts:37-39), with no header row and no blank separator, diverging from the documented shape (README.md:52-54) and from the normal empty-result output (header + '' + '0 rows').

The trigger is narrow: columns is empty only when runQuery's statement.columns() itself throws and the catch sets columns=[] (database.ts:49-53); ordinary SELECTs always report >=1 column. So it is a genuine but low-frequency consistency/documentation inconsistency.
Correctly categorised as documentation/consistency; minor.
issue-evidence: src/format.ts:37-39  if (columns.length === 0) { return "0 rows"; }
README.md:52-54 documents the header + blank line + row count shape
src/format.ts:43 normal path returns [header, ...body, '', footer].join('\n')
issues.review-evidence: format.ts:37-39 `if (columns.length === 0) return "0 rows";` vs line 43 `[header, ...body, "", footer].join("\n")`. README.md:52-54 documents header + blank line + row count. Divergence is real; reachability is an edge case (columns() must throw).
issues.title: Empty-column result diverges from documented TSV shape
issues.description: toTSV emits a bare '0 rows' with no header/blank line when columns are unknown, inconsistent with the documented format and the normal empty output.
fixes: src/format.ts: removed the special-case bare '0 rows'; toTSV now always emits [header, ...body, '', footer], so a zero-column result degenerates to an empty header line + blank line + count, matching the documented TSV shape.

issue-observations: describeTable wraps the pragma_table_info(?) attempt in a bare `catch {}` that swallows every error and falls back to string-interpolated PRAGMA table_info(...). The catch is intended only for 'table-valued function unavailable', but it also masks any other failure (corruption, out-of-memory, prepare errors), silently switching code paths.
review-observations: Confirmed: the try/catch around the pragma_table_info(?) table-valued path uses a bare `catch {}` that swallows every error and unconditionally switches to the string-interpolated PRAGMA table_info fallback (database.ts:86-107).

The intended trigger is only 'table-valued function unavailable', but any error (corruption, OOM, prepare failure) silently changes code path. In practice a genuinely broken DB would also fail the fallback and re-throw, limiting the harm, so severity is low.
Reasonable 'maintainability' finding; the fallback pattern itself is sound but the catch could be narrowed.
issue-evidence: src/database.ts:86-99  try { ...pragma_table_info(?)... } catch { const quoted = ...; const statement = prepare(db, `PRAGMA table_info(${quoted})`); ... }
issues.review-evidence: database.ts:86-99: `try { ...pragma_table_info(?)... } catch { const quoted=...; prepare(db, `PRAGMA table_info(${quoted})`) ... }` — no error inspection in the catch. Confirmed by file read.
issues.title: Overly broad catch in describeTable masks unrelated errors
issues.description: The catch-all around the table-valued-function path silently triggers the fallback for any error, not just the unsupported-feature case it targets.
fixes: src/database.ts: describeTable's fallback catch now rethrows unless the error is a SQLite compile error (code ERR_SQLITE_ERROR), so only the unavailable-table-valued-function case triggers the PRAGMA fallback; corruption/OOM propagate instead of silently switching paths.

issue-observations: escapeCell escapes backslash, tab, CR and LF but passes through all other control characters (NUL, vertical tab, form feed, escape, etc.). SQLite BLOBs are hex-encoded, but a TEXT value containing such control bytes is emitted raw into the TSV, which can corrupt terminal display or confuse a downstream parser.
review-observations: The factual observation is true: escapeCell escapes only backslash, tab, CR and LF, so other control characters (NUL, VT, FF, ESC, etc.) in TEXT values pass through raw.

But categorising this as a 'bug' overstates impact. The TSV structural delimiters (tab, CR, LF) ARE escaped, so the output remains unambiguously parseable by a tab/newline splitter; raw control bytes are faithful content, not structural corruption or data loss.

Correct read: minor robustness/rendering nit, not a correctness bug on par with the duplicate-column data loss. Terminal-display 'corruption' is a rendering concern, and a compliant TSV parser is unaffected.
issue-evidence: src/format.ts:22-26  return text.replace(/\\/g, '\\\\').replace(/\t/g, '\\t').replace(/\r/g, '\\r').replace(/\n/g, '\\n');
issues.review-evidence: Repro: applying the four .replace() chains from format.ts:22-26 to 'a\x00b\x0bc\x0cd\x1be' leaves NUL/VT/FF/ESC untouched. However none of these are TSV delimiters, so field/line structure is preserved. The finding is real but 'bug' severity is overstated; it is a minor robustness matter.
issues.title: Non tab/CR/LF control characters passed through unescaped
issues.description: escapeCell only escapes \\, \t, \r, \n; other control characters in TEXT values reach the output raw and may corrupt rendering/parsing.
fixes: src/format.ts: escapeCell now escapes remaining control characters (C0 controls plus DEL) as \xNN, reversibly (backslash is escaped first); README output-format note updated to document it.

issue-observations: openDatabase silently resolves relative paths against process.cwd() (resolve(process.cwd(), path)), even though every tool description states the path must be absolute and the input schema does not enforce it. For an MCP stdio server the cwd is the client's launch directory, which is unpredictable, so a relative or empty path can open an unintended file or directory rather than failing clearly.
An empty-string path resolves to the cwd itself (a directory), producing an obscure open error.
review-observations: The relative-path resolution is explicitly documented and deliberate: database.ts:11-12 states 'Relative paths are resolved against the server process's working directory as a best effort, but callers should pass an absolute path.'

This aligns with the intentional trust-the-user, no-sandbox, per-call-path design. The 'must be absolute' text is a soft guideline, and the lenient resolve() is an intended best-effort fallback, not an accident.
The empty-string-resolves-to-cwd edge produces an obscure open error, but that is a benign consequence of the documented behaviour for a local trust-the-user tool; not a defect to act on.
issue-evidence: src/database.ts:14-17  const location = isAbsolute(path) ? path : resolve(process.cwd(), path); return new DatabaseSync(location, { readOnly: true });
src/index.ts:65 path described as 'Absolute path ...' but schema is only z.string()
issues.review-evidence: database.ts:14-17 `const location = isAbsolute(path) ? path : resolve(process.cwd(), path); return new DatabaseSync(location, { readOnly: true });` with the docstring at lines 10-13 explicitly describing this as intended best-effort behaviour. Matches the stated intentional design (per-call path, no validation/sandbox).
issues.title: Relative paths silently resolved against unpredictable cwd
issues.description: Despite the 'must be absolute' contract, relative/empty paths are quietly resolved against the server's cwd, which can open the wrong file.
fixes: no code change: review-outcome intentional-behaviour (documented best-effort relative-path resolution; trust-the-user design).

issue-observations: main() only connects the transport and returns; there is no handling of transport close, no SIGINT/SIGTERM shutdown, and no error handler on the server/transport after connect(). Process lifetime and clean shutdown depend entirely on undocumented StdioServerTransport behaviour.
review-observations: Factually true that main() only connects the transport with no SIGINT/SIGTERM handler and no post-connect error/close handling.

But the 'architecture' framing overstates impact: database handles are opened per call and closed in a finally block (index.ts:44-49), so there is no long-lived resource that a shutdown hook would need to release. StdioServerTransport closing on stdin EOF is the standard MCP stdio lifecycle.
Correct read: a very low-severity nice-to-have, not an architectural defect; relying on the SDK transport for lifecycle is idiomatic and consistent with the intentional client-agnostic design.
issue-evidence: src/index.ts:116-124 main() { const transport = new StdioServerTransport(); await server.connect(transport); }  main().catch(...)
issues.review-evidence: index.ts:116-124 main() creates StdioServerTransport, awaits server.connect(transport); top-level main().catch logs and exits. No persistent DB/file handle survives a call (withDatabase closes in finally), so there is nothing to clean up on signal. Impact is minimal; category overstated.
issues.title: No explicit shutdown / transport-close / signal handling
issues.description: The server relies solely on the SDK transport for lifecycle; there is no signal handling or post-connect error handling for graceful shutdown.
fixes: src/index.ts: main() now installs SIGINT/SIGTERM handlers that call server.close() then process.exit(0) (light touch; DB handles are opened/closed per call so there is no long-lived state to unwind).

issue-observations: The round-1 refactor split withDatabase into an open-catch that runs describeError and a separate fn-catch that returns the raw error message. This assumes file-validity errors (not-a-database / malformed / encrypted) surface while openDatabase() runs. But new DatabaseSync(path, { readOnly: true }) opens LAZILY: constructing it against a non-SQLite (or corrupt/encrypted) file does NOT throw. The error is thrown at the first prepare()/all(), i.e. inside fn(), so it hits the raw fn-catch (index.ts:66-69), never describeError.

Consequence 1 (behaviour regression): a caller pointed at an existing-but-invalid file now gets the bare engine message 'Error: file is not a database' with no path context, instead of the intended 'Error: "<path>" is not a valid SQLite database file.' Before round-1's fix, fn() errors also flowed through describeError, so this friendlier message WAS produced.

Consequence 2 (dead code): describeError's second branch — /not a database|file is encrypted|malformed/i -> '"${path}" is not a valid SQLite database file.' (index.ts:45-47) — is now unreachable for all three tools, because that error class only ever arises at query time (lazy open), which bypasses describeError.

The missing-file case still works because a NON-EXISTENT path DOES throw at construction ('unable to open database file'), so describeError's first branch is reachable; only the invalid/corrupt-file branch is dead.
review-observations: Upheld. Confirmed empirically that new DatabaseSync(path, { readOnly: true }) opens lazily: constructing against an existing non-SQLite file does NOT throw; the 'file is not a database' error (code ERR_SQLITE_ERROR) is raised at the first prepare()/all() inside fn(), so it hits withDatabase's raw fn-catch (index.ts:66-69), never describeError.

Regression confirmed end-to-end through the dist MCP server: list_tables on an existing non-SQLite file returns 'Error: file is not a database' (bare, no path context), whereas a MISSING path throws 'unable to open database file' at construction, hits describeError's first branch, and yields the friendly 'could not open ...' message.

Dead-code claim verified: no construction-time error matches describeError's second branch /not a database|file is encrypted|malformed/i. Plain-text and SQLite-magic+garbage files defer to query time (message would match the SECOND branch, but that path bypasses describeError); a directory throws 'disk I/O error' at open (matches NEITHER branch); only a missing file throws at open (FIRST branch). So index.ts:45-47 is unreachable for all three tools.

This is a genuine regression from the round-1 split (iteration 1 issue index 3), which moved fn() errors out of describeError's reach; before that change the query-time 'not a database' error flowed through describeError and produced the friendly message. Category 'bug' (behaviour regression + dead branch) is appropriate. Cited line numbers 66-69 and 45-47 are accurate.
issue-evidence: src/index.ts:66-69  try { return fn(db); } catch (error) { return errorResult(error instanceof Error ? error.message : String(error)); }  // raw, no describeError
src/index.ts:45-47  if (/not a database|file is encrypted|malformed/i.test(message)) { return `"${path}" is not a valid SQLite database file.`; }  // now unreachable
Probe (node v22.21.1): new DatabaseSync('/tmp/notadb', {readOnly:true}) -> 'construction: OK (lazy, did NOT throw)'; first query threw 'file is not a database' code=ERR_SQLITE_ERROR.

End-to-end via dist server: list_tables on an existing non-SQLite file returns text 'Error: file is not a database' (raw, no path); on a MISSING file returns 'Error: could not open "..." ...' (friendly, open-time).
issues.review-evidence: Probe (node v22.21.1): writeFileSync(bad,'plain text'); new DatabaseSync(bad,{readOnly:true}) -> CONSTRUCT OK (lazy, no throw); first prepare('SELECT name FROM sqlite_master').all() -> THREW code=ERR_SQLITE_ERROR 'file is not a database'. Missing path -> CONSTRUCT THREW code=ERR_SQLITE_ERROR 'unable to open database file'. Directory path -> CONSTRUCT THREW 'disk I/O error'. E2E via dist/index.js over MCP stdio: list_tables{path:non-sqlite-file} -> 'Error: file is not a database' (isError, no path); list_tables{path:missing} -> 'Error: could not open "..." — file not found or not readable...'; query{path:non-sqlite-file} -> 'Error: file is not a database'. describeError's second branch is never reached because the not-a-database/malformed/encrypted class only surfaces at query time (lazy open), which hits the raw fn-catch.
issues.title: not-a-database errors bypass describeError (regression + dead branch)
issues.description: Because readOnly open is lazy, invalid/corrupt/encrypted-file errors surface at query time and hit withDatabase's raw fn-catch, so users get bare 'file is not a database' and describeError's not-a-valid-database branch is unreachable dead code — a regression from round-1's open/query error split.
fixes: src/database.ts: openDatabase now runs a cheap `PRAGMA schema_version` probe immediately after constructing the read-only handle, so a non-SQLite/corrupt/encrypted file fails at open time (not lazily at first query) and its error is classified by describeError; the handle is closed before the probe error is rethrown. A missing file still throws at construction; a valid file passes the probe.

src/index.ts: describeError's `/not a database|file is encrypted|malformed/i` branch is reachable again because file-level errors now surface through openDatabase()/the open-catch — a non-SQLite file yields the friendly path-including message while genuine SQL-execution errors still return their raw message. No dead code left behind.

issue-observations: The suite imports only dist/database.js and dist/format.js. Nothing imports dist/index.js, so withDatabase, describeError, textResult/errorResult and the three registered tool handlers are entirely untested. In particular describeError's regex classification (file-not-found vs not-a-valid-database) — the logic most likely to silently break and which IS currently broken per the finding above — has zero coverage; a test asserting the tool's error text for a missing file and for a non-SQLite file would have caught that regression.

describeTable's PRAGMA table_info(...) fallback branch (database.ts:142-161, the catch path) is never exercised: on the supported runtime the pragma_table_info(?) table-valued function always succeeds, so the fallback's positional [row[1]..row[5]] remap and its boolean conversion are unverified.
The describe_table handler's 'no such table or view' error path (index.ts:110-113) and openDatabase's error handling are untested.

Note the self-join test only asserts row.length===4, not the row VALUES, so it would silently pass even on the object-mode fallback path (which corrupts values); only the aliased-column test checks values and would catch that path.
review-observations: Upheld. test/ contains only database.test.js and format.test.js; grep -rn 'index' test/ returns nothing, so index.ts (withDatabase, describeError, textResult/errorResult, and all three tool handlers) has zero coverage.
Confirmed the describeError classification gap is exactly what would have caught the issue-index-0 regression: there is no assertion on the tool's error text for a missing file vs a non-SQLite file.

PRAGMA table_info(...) fallback branch (database.ts:142-161) is unreachable on the test runtime because pragma_table_info(?) succeeds (verified: it returns rows on v22.21.1) and no test forces the fallback, so the positional [row[1]..row[5]] remap is unexercised. Nuance: the shared boolean pk conversion at 162-164 IS covered by the composite test, but only via the primary path.

Verified the self-join test (database.test.js:26-36) asserts only columns, rows.length and each row.length===4 — not values — so it would still pass on the object-mode fallback (columns.map yields 4 entries regardless); only the aliased-column test (deepEqual rows[0], ['a','a','n1']) would catch object-mode value collapse. describeTable('nonexistent') returning rows.length===0 (verified from dist) and the handler's 'no such table' path (index.ts:110-113) are untested. Accurate test-gap finding, correctly categorised.
issue-evidence: test/database.test.js:7  import { openDatabase, runQuery, listTables, describeTable } from "../dist/database.js";  (no import of ../dist/index.js anywhere in test/)
test/format.test.js:3  import { toTSV } from "../dist/format.js";
src/database.ts:142-161 fallback catch branch — unreachable on the test runtime, therefore untested
test/database.test.js:26-36 self-join test asserts only rows.length and row.length, not values
issues.review-evidence: ls test/ -> database.test.js, format.test.js only. grep -rn 'index' test/ -> no matches. database.test.js:7 imports from ../dist/database.js; format.test.js:3 imports from ../dist/format.js — nothing imports ../dist/index.js. pragma_table_info(?) verified working (returns rows on node v22.21.1), so describeTable's fallback catch (database.ts:142-161) never triggers under the tests. describeTable(db,'does_not_exist') from dist -> rows.length===0, untested. Self-join test asserts row.length (===4) not row values; only the aliased-column test checks values.
issues.title: Tests never cover index.ts (describeError/handlers) or the PRAGMA fallback
issues.description: The added tests exercise database.ts and format.ts only; index.ts error classification, tool handlers and describeTable's PRAGMA fallback branch are untested, so the describeError regression and the fallback remap are unguarded.
fixes: test/e2e.test.js: new end-to-end suite spawns dist/index.js over MCP stdio with the SDK Client + StdioClientTransport and asserts list_tables/describe_table/query happy paths, the friendly error for a MISSING file and for a NON-SQLite file (guards issue #1), the 'no such table or view' error, read-only write rejection, the empty-SQL error, and boolean not_null/primary_key in describe_table output.

test/database.test.js: strengthened the self-join test to assert the actual VALUES of all four duplicate-named columns (not just row/column counts), added a not_null-boolean assertion consistent with primary_key, and added a skipped test documenting that describeTable's PRAGMA table_info fallback is unreachable on Node >=22.16 (pragma_table_info(?) always succeeds), rather than forcing it.

issue-observations: runQuery's try/catch wraps only prepare(db, sql); the very next line, statement.columns() (database.ts:84), is OUTSIDE the guarded region. The friendly empty/comment-only-SQL error is produced only because prepare() internally calls statement.setReadBigInts(true) FIRST (database.ts:38-40), and that call throws ERR_INVALID_STATE for a no-statement input while still inside the guarded prepare().

Empirically db.prepare('') does NOT throw — it returns a finalized statement, and the first method call on it throws. The runQuery comment (database.ts:74-78) states node:sqlite 'reports an opaque "statement has been finalized" ... when the input compiles to no statement', implying prepare() itself reports it; that is not what happens.

This is a fragile, undocumented coupling: setReadBigInts is @since v22.5.0 so it is always present on supported Node (>=22.16), and the detection is reliable today. But a maintainer who deferred/removed the setter calls from prepare(), or reordered them after columns(), would cause the unguarded statement.columns() at line 84 to throw the raw 'statement has been finalized', defeating isNoStatementError and leaking the opaque message the round-1 fix was written to eliminate.
review-observations: Upheld. Verified db.prepare('') (and '   ', '-- comment', '/* x */', newline+tab) does NOT throw — it returns a statement. The ERR_INVALID_STATE 'statement has been finalized' is thrown by the subsequent statement.setReadBigInts(true) call inside prepare() (database.ts:38-40), which is within runQuery's guarded try, so isNoStatementError classifies it and the friendly 'no SQL statement to run' error is produced.

Confirmed statement.columns() at database.ts:84 is OUTSIDE the try/catch (lines 71-83 wrap only prepare). columns() throws the same ERR_INVALID_STATE. So the friendly message depends entirely on a setter call throwing first inside prepare(); if a maintainer removed/deferred setReadBigInts/setReturnArrays or reordered them after columns(), the unguarded columns() would throw the raw 'statement has been finalized' uncaught by runQuery, propagate to withDatabase's fn-catch, and leak the opaque message the fix targets.

The runQuery comment (database.ts:74-78) says node:sqlite 'reports an opaque ...statement has been finalized... when the input compiles to no statement', implying prepare() reports it; in fact prepare() returns cleanly and a method call on the finalized statement reports it — a minor mis-description. Reliable today (setters @since v22.5.0, always present on Node >=22.16) but a fragile, undocumented coupling. Correctly categorised maintainability, low severity.
issue-evidence: src/database.ts:71-83  try { statement = prepare(db, sql); } catch (error) { if (isNoStatementError(error)) throw new Error('no SQL statement to run: ...'); throw error; }
src/database.ts:84  const columns = statement.columns().map((column) => column.name);  // outside the try/catch
src/database.ts:38-40  prepare() calls statement.setReadBigInts(true) before returning
Probe (node v22.21.1): db.prepare('') -> OK (no throw); st.setReadBigInts(true) -> threw code=ERR_INVALID_STATE 'statement has been finalized'; st.columns() also throws the same.
issues.review-evidence: Probe (node v22.21.1): db.prepare('') -> OK (no throw); st.setReadBigInts(true) -> THREW code=ERR_INVALID_STATE 'statement has been finalized'; st.columns() -> THREW code=ERR_INVALID_STATE 'statement has been finalized'. Same for '   ', '-- comment', '/* x */', newline+tab. Source: runQuery's try wraps only prepare() (database.ts:71-83); columns() at line 84 is unguarded. isNoStatementError matches code ERR_INVALID_STATE, but only the error thrown inside prepare() (via the setter) reaches the catch.
issues.title: Empty-SQL detection coupled to prepare()'s setReadBigInts side effect
issues.description: The no-statement error is caught only because prepare() calls setReadBigInts inside the guarded try; statement.columns() at line 84 is unguarded, so any refactor that defers the setter calls would leak the raw 'statement has been finalized' the fix targets, and the code comment misdescribes the mechanism.
fixes: src/database.ts: runQuery now guards the whole prepare()+columns() sequence in a single try/catch (columns() was previously outside it), so the no-compilable-statement case is classified by isNoStatementError wherever the ERR_INVALID_STATE trips — independent of prepare()'s setReadBigInts side effect — and still emits the friendly 'no SQL statement to run' message.

src/database.ts: corrected the misleading comment to describe the real mechanism (prepare() returns a statement that compiled to nothing; the opaque error is thrown by the first method call on it, not by prepare() itself).

issue-observations: describe_table now renders primary_key as a JS boolean (true/false) but leaves not_null as the raw PRAGMA integer 0/1, producing an inconsistent presentation of two conceptually-boolean columns in the same output. Round-1 converted pk to boolean (database.ts:162-164) but did not touch not_null.
Verified output for CREATE TABLE u(id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, email TEXT NOT NULL, nick TEXT): the not_null column shows 0/1 while primary_key shows true/false side by side.
The tool description advertises 'nullability' (not a boolean flag) so this is defensible, but the mixed 0/1-and-true/false shape is a minor readability/consistency wart a consumer must special-case.
review-observations: Upheld (low severity). Confirmed from dist describeTable('u'): primary_key renders as JS boolean true/false while not_null renders as the raw PRAGMA integer (actually a BigInt 0n/1n under setReadBigInts, printed as '0'/'1'); the two boolean-like columns appear side by side in inconsistent shapes.
Round-1's conversion loop (database.ts:162-164) touches only PK_INDEX (primary_key); not_null (index 2) is left untouched. Verified.

Partly defensible: the tool description calls primary_key 'a boolean primary-key flag' but describes not_null only as 'nullability', and PRAGMA notnull is genuinely 0/1, so surfacing it raw is arguable. But the asymmetry is a real minor presentation/consistency wart a consumer must special-case. Correctly categorised api-design, low severity; it objects to no stated intentional decision, so in scope.
issue-evidence: src/database.ts:162-164  for (const row of rows) { row[PK_INDEX] = Number(row[PK_INDEX]) !== 0; }  // only primary_key converted
Probe output: header name/type/not_null/default_value/primary_key; rows: id INTEGER 0 (blank) true ; email TEXT 1 (blank) false ; nick TEXT 0 (blank) false
issues.review-evidence: From dist (node v22.21.1), CREATE TABLE u(id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, email TEXT NOT NULL, nick TEXT); describeTable TSV rows: id -> not_null=0, primary_key=true; email -> not_null=1, primary_key=false; nick -> not_null=0, primary_key=false. typeof not_null === 'bigint' (0n/1n), typeof primary_key === 'boolean'. database.ts:162-164 converts only row[PK_INDEX] (=4), leaving not_null (index 2) as the raw PRAGMA value.
issues.title: not_null rendered as 0/1 while primary_key is boolean
issues.description: describe_table shows primary_key as true/false but not_null as raw 0/1 in the same table, an inconsistent presentation of two boolean-like columns left after round-1 converted only pk.
fixes: src/database.ts: describeTable now converts not_null (PRAGMA notnull, 0/1) into a real boolean via a NOT_NULL_INDEX conversion alongside primary_key, for both the table-valued and fallback paths, so the two boolean-like columns present consistently; updated the JSDoc.
src/index.ts + README.md: describe_table tool description and README now document not_null as a boolean not-null flag (true when the column is NOT NULL), matching the emitted shape.

issue-observations: prepare() feature-detects setReadBigInts and setReturnArrays via `typeof === 'function'` (database.ts:56-61) and the JSDoc states this is 'so the module still loads on a runtime that predates them.' But runQuery calls `statement.columns()` unconditionally at database.ts:92 with no such guard.

Per the official Node v22.x docs, StatementSync.columns() and setReturnArrays() were BOTH added in v22.16.0, while setReadBigInts() dates to v22.5.0. So on any runtime old enough to lack setReturnArrays (< 22.16.0) columns() is equally absent, and runQuery's unguarded columns() would throw 'statement.columns is not a function' before readRows ever runs.

That TypeError is not matched by isNoStatementError (it has no ERR_INVALID_STATE code and no 'statement has been finalized' message, database.ts:112-116), so it propagates to withDatabase's fn-catch as a bare, confusing 'Error: statement.columns is not a function'. The feature-detection thus gives a false impression of support for pre-22.16 runtimes, and readRows' object-mode fallback is unreachable via runQuery (columns() throws first).

Real-world impact is negligible because engines.node is >=22.16.0 (exactly the release that shipped columns()/setReturnArrays), so on every supported runtime all three methods exist; this is an internal inconsistency plus a slightly misleading comment, not a live crash.
review-observations: Confirmed the inconsistency by reading current source: prepare() feature-detects both setters via `typeof === "function"` (database.ts:56-61) with the comment "so the module still loads on a runtime that predates them", yet runQuery calls statement.columns() unconditionally at database.ts:92 with no guard.

Verified the Node version premise against the official v22.x docs: statement.columns() and statement.setReturnArrays() are BOTH "Added in: v22.16.0", while setReadBigInts() is "Added in: v22.5.0". So on any runtime old enough to lack setReturnArrays, columns() is equally absent, and the unguarded columns() on line 92 would throw "statement.columns is not a function" before the setReturnArrays guard buys anything.

Confirmed isNoStatementError (database.ts:112-116) would NOT catch that: empirically a missing statement method throws a TypeError with code=undefined and message "... is not a function", which matches neither the ERR_INVALID_STATE code check nor /statement has been finalized/i, so it would propagate raw to withDatabase's fn-catch.

Impact is negligible in practice: package.json engines is ">=22.16.0" (the exact release that shipped columns()/setReturnArrays), so on every supported runtime all three methods exist and none of the guards ever fire. This is a genuine internal guard/comment inconsistency, not a live crash, and is honestly scoped as low-severity maintainability. Worth a small consistency tidy (guard columns() too, or drop the now-moot feature-detection given the floor).
issue-evidence: src/database.ts:56-61 guard setReadBigInts/setReturnArrays; src/database.ts:92 `columns = statement.columns().map((column) => column.name)` is unconditional

Node v22.x API docs (nodejs.org/docs/latest-v22.x/api/sqlite.html): statement.columns() 'Added in: v22.16.0', statement.setReturnArrays() 'Added in: v22.16.0', statement.setReadBigInts() 'Added in: v22.5.0'
src/database.ts:112-116 isNoStatementError matches only code ERR_INVALID_STATE or /statement has been finalized/i; a missing-method TypeError falls through and is rethrown raw
issues.review-evidence: Code: database.ts:56-61 guard setReadBigInts/setReturnArrays with typeof checks; database.ts:92 calls statement.columns() unguarded; database.ts:112-116 isNoStatementError matches only code ERR_INVALID_STATE or /statement has been finalized/i. Node v22.x docs (nodejs.org/docs/latest-v22.x/api/sqlite.html) confirm columns() and setReturnArrays() are both "Added in: v22.16.0" and setReadBigInts() is "Added in: v22.5.0" -- columns() and setReturnArrays() share the same 22.16.0 origin. Probe (node v22.21.1): calling a nonexistent StatementSync method throws name=TypeError, code=undefined, msg="... is not a function", which isNoStatementError does not match. package.json engines.node=">=22.16.0", so all three methods are always present on supported runtimes and the discrepancy never surfaces as a crash; it is a real, low-severity maintainability inconsistency plus a slightly misleading backward-compat comment.
issues.title: columns() called unconditionally while the two setters are feature-detected
issues.description: runQuery calls statement.columns() with no feature guard, yet prepare() guards setReadBigInts/setReturnArrays 'so the module still loads on a runtime that predates them'; columns() shares setReturnArrays's exact 22.16.0 origin, so the guard is inconsistent and the backward-compat intent is not met for the query/list_tables path.
fixes: src/database.ts: dropped the typeof feature-detection guards (and the now-inaccurate optional cast) in prepare(), calling statement.setReadBigInts(true)/setReturnArrays(true) directly.

src/database.ts: rewrote the prepare() JSDoc to state these setters and columns() are all guaranteed present by the >=22.16.0 Node engines floor, so no feature-detection is needed — making prepare() and the unguarded columns() consistent.

issue-observations: README.md:57 says 'Tabs, newlines and backslashes inside values are escaped (`\t`, `\n`, `\\`)' and README.md:58 says 'Other control characters are escaped as `\xNN` (e.g. NUL as `\x00`).' Carriage return (CR, 0x0d) is neither in the first list nor covered by the \xNN rule.

escapeCell actually renders CR as `\r` (format.ts:35), and the \xNN regex (format.ts:37) explicitly excludes 0x0d. A reader would wrongly infer a CR byte is emitted raw or as `\x0d`; it is actually `\r`. Minor doc inaccuracy/omission.
review-observations: Confirmed: README.md:57 lists only `\t`, `\n`, `\\` ("Tabs, newlines and backslashes ... escaped"), and README.md:58 says "Other control characters are escaped as `\xNN`". Carriage return appears in neither.

escapeCell escapes CR as `\r` (format.ts:35 `.replace(/\r/g, "\\r")`), and the trailing \xNN regex (format.ts:37, range `[\x00-\x08\x0b\x0c\x0e-\x1f\x7f]`) deliberately excludes 0x0d, so CR is emitted as `\r` -- not `\x0d` and not raw. The doc both omits `\r` from the explicit list and misleads by implying CR would fall under the \xNN rule.

Existing test format.test.js:45-47 confirms the CR->`\r` behaviour (asserts "a\\tb\\nc\\rd\\\\e" for input containing a CR). Genuine, accurate documentation gap, low severity. Not a duplicate of the iteration-1 control-char finding: that fix ADDED the \xNN bullet (README:58); this points out the same update never documented the pre-existing CR->`\r` escape.
issue-evidence: README.md:57-58 escaping bullets list \t, \n, \\ then 'Other control characters ... \xNN' (no mention of CR / \r)
src/format.ts:35 `.replace(/\r/g, "\\r")` escapes CR as \r; format.ts:37 regex `[\x00-\x08\x0b\x0c\x0e-\x1f\x7f]` deliberately excludes 0x0d
issues.review-evidence: README.md:57 "Tabs, newlines and backslashes inside values are escaped (`\t`, `\n`, `\\`)." README.md:58 "Other control characters are escaped as `\xNN` (e.g. NUL as `\x00`)." -- CR is absent from both, and the \xNN framing wrongly implies CR would be \x0d. format.ts:35 escapes CR as `\r`; format.ts:37's regex range excludes 0x0d so CR never reaches the \xNN path. Confirmed by the passing test format.test.js:45-47 whose input contains a real CR and expects `\r`. Real doc omission/inaccuracy, low severity, correctly categorised documentation.
issues.title: Output-format doc omits carriage-return escaping
issues.description: The README escaping bullets list \t, \n, \\ and say 'other control characters are escaped as \xNN', but carriage return is escaped as \r and is neither listed nor covered by the \xNN rule.
fixes: README.md: added carriage return to the escaping bullet (now lists \t, \r, \n, \\), matching escapeCell in src/format.ts which renders CR as \r.

issue-observations: describeError has exactly two branches: file-not-found (/unable to open|ENOENT|no such file|cannot open/i) and not-a-valid-database (/not a database|file is encrypted|malformed/i); anything else returns the raw engine message (index.ts:42-48).

Verified: passing a `path` that is a directory makes the openDatabase probe (or the constructor) throw SQLite's 'disk I/O error', which matches neither branch, so the caller gets bare 'Error: disk I/O error' with no path and no guidance — inconsistent with the friendly, path-including messages produced for a missing file and a non-SQLite file.
Low severity and an edge case, but it is a genuine consistency gap in how open errors are surfaced (in scope: about error classification, not the no-sandbox decision).
review-observations: Empirically confirmed (node v22.21.1): new DatabaseSync('<a directory>', {readOnly:true}) throws ERR_SQLITE_ERROR with message "disk I/O error" AT CONSTRUCTION (before openDatabase's PRAGMA schema_version probe even runs), so it is thrown inside openDatabase and caught by withDatabase's open-catch -> describeError.

describeError (index.ts:40-48) has exactly two regex branches -- /unable to open|ENOENT|no such file|cannot open/i and /not a database|file is encrypted|malformed/i -- then `return message`. "disk I/O error" matches neither, so a directory path yields the bare "Error: disk I/O error" with no path and no guidance, whereas a missing file and a non-SQLite file both get friendly, path-including messages. Verified by running all three real engine messages through a faithful re-implementation of describeError.

In scope: this is about error classification (describeError's documented job is "Turn a low-level SQLite/filesystem error into a message the model can act on"), not the intentional no-sandbox/trust-the-user design. For a plausible mistaken input (a directory, or a permission-denied path) the classifier fails its own contract -- a real, low-severity consistency gap worth a small fall-through fix. The "bug" label is at the generous end (the raw error is still surfaced), but the asymmetry against the two friendly cases is genuine; severity is not overstated (the finding itself says low/edge-case).

Distinct from iteration-2 issue #0: that raised the lazy-open not-a-database bypass (since fixed by the PRAGMA probe). The disk-I/O-error/directory case was only noted in passing during that review, never raised as its own finding, so this is not a duplicate.
issue-evidence: Probe (node v22.21.1): new DatabaseSync('<a directory>', {readOnly:true}) throws code=ERR_SQLITE_ERROR msg='disk I/O error' at construction
src/index.ts:40-48 describeError: two regex branches then `return message` (raw fall-through)
issues.review-evidence: Probe (node v22.21.1): `new DatabaseSync(<dir>, {readOnly:true})` -> THREW code=ERR_SQLITE_ERROR msg="disk I/O error" at construction; missing path -> "unable to open database file"; non-SQLite file -> "file is not a database" (at the probe). Feeding these through a faithful re-implementation of describeError (index.ts:40-48): "disk I/O error" -> RAW passthrough (no path); "unable to open database file" -> friendly "could not open <path>"; "file is not a database" -> friendly "<path> is not a valid SQLite database file". So the directory case genuinely bypasses both friendly branches and returns the bare engine message. Low severity, in scope (error classification, not the no-sandbox decision); not a duplicate of the already-fixed iteration-2 lazy-open finding.
issues.title: Open errors other than missing / not-a-db return the bare engine message
issues.description: describeError classifies only file-not-found and not-a-valid-database; any other open failure (e.g. a directory path, which SQLite reports as 'disk I/O error') falls through to the raw engine message with no path context, unlike the two friendly cases.
fixes: src/index.ts: added a final fallback in describeError that prefixes the raw engine message with the path (could not open "<path>": <message>), so an unclassified open error such as a directory's "disk I/O error" keeps path context; only the open path calls describeError, so SQL-execution errors are unaffected.

issue-observations: describe_table is documented to work on 'one table or view' (README.md:47), but describeTable is only ever called with tables ('t', 'composite', 'u' in database.test.js; 'u' in e2e.test.js); no test describes a view. Verified manually that it works, but the documented view path is unguarded against regression.

BigInt and BLOB values appear only as literal inputs to toTSV in test/format.test.js; no test issues a real query returning an integer > Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER or a BLOB, so runQuery's setReadBigInts wiring and the whole read path for those types are unverified end to end.
review-observations: Confirmed no view is ever exercised: database.test.js seeds only tables t/composite/u and calls describeTable with "composite" and "u"; e2e.test.js seeds t/u and calls describe_table with "u". Neither suite issues CREATE VIEW (grep for view/CREATE VIEW in test/ returns nothing), so the documented "table or view" capability (README.md:47) has no describe-a-view coverage.

Confirmed BigInt/BLOB are only synthetic toTSV inputs (format.test.js:34-43): 9007199254740993n and a Uint8Array are passed straight to toTSV. No test runs an actual query that RETURNS an integer > Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER or a BLOB -- table u (the only INTEGER PRIMARY KEY table) is created but never INSERTed in either suite -- so runQuery's setReadBigInts wiring and the end-to-end read path for those types are unverified.

Regression-relevant and worth a small fix: if the setReadBigInts(true) call in prepare() were removed, a large-integer column would throw ERR_OUT_OF_RANGE and no current test would catch it. Genuine test gap, correctly categorised test-gap, low severity. Not a duplicate of iteration-2 issue #1 (which was about index.ts / PRAGMA-fallback coverage, since addressed by the e2e suite).
issue-evidence: test/database.test.js / test/e2e.test.js: describeTable/describe_table invoked only on tables, never a view
test/format.test.js:34-43 BigInt/BLOB tested only as synthetic toTSV inputs; no runQuery/query-tool test exercises them
issues.review-evidence: test/database.test.js before() creates tables t (2 rows), composite (0 rows), u (0 rows) -- no CREATE VIEW; describeTable called with "composite","u" only. test/e2e.test.js creates t,u -- no view; describe_table called with "u". grep of test/ finds no CREATE VIEW and no BLOB-returning query; INSERT statements only ever target table t, so u is never populated and no SELECT returns a BigInt or BLOB. test/format.test.js:34-43 exercises BigInt (9007199254740993n) and a BLOB (Uint8Array) only as literal toTSV inputs. Therefore describe_table on a view, and the setReadBigInts/BLOB read path through runQuery / the query tool, are untested end-to-end. Confirmed by full read of all three test files. Low-severity test-gap, in scope.
issues.title: No test for describing a view or for BigInt/BLOB through the query path
issues.description: describe_table on a VIEW (a documented capability) is never tested, and BigInt/BLOB rendering is only unit-tested against synthetic toTSV inputs — no test runs an actual query that returns a large integer or a BLOB, so the setReadBigInts wiring and end-to-end value flow are unverified.
fixes: test/database.test.js: seeded a VIEW and a table holding an integer > 2^53 plus a BLOB; added tests that listTables/describeTable cover the view and that a real runQuery preserves the BigInt (typeof bigint, exact value) and returns the BLOB as a Uint8Array.

test/e2e.test.js: seeded the same view and big-integer/BLOB row; added tests that list_tables/describe_table work on the view and that the query tool returns the BigInt intact and the BLOB rendered as x'deadbeef' in the TSV output.
